coredata.dc:title: Structure-performance relationships between amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots and self-cleaning nanofiltration membranes

coredata.dc:description: To develop self-cleaning nanofiltration (NF) membranes with excellent desalination performance, five kinds of amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots (AA-fGQDs) were synthesized and introduced into the active layers of the thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes via the interfacial polymerization (IP) method. The synthesized AA-fGQDs and the resulting TFN NF membranes were systematically characterized to investigate the structure-performance relationships between the AA-fGQDs and the NF membranes. The results showed that the desalination and photocatalytic self-cleaning performance of the fabricated TFN membranes were closely related to the types and structures of AA-fGQDs. The TFN membranes incorporating AA-fGQDs with the polar amino acids exhibit better desalination performance than those incorporating AA-fGQDs with the non-polar amino acids, overcoming the trade-off effect between water flux and salt rejection. The TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane exhibits superior water permeability of 27.35 LMH·bar−1 while maintaining high salt rejection (97.95% for Na2SO4), equal to 4.41 times of the TFC-blank membrane (6.20 LMH·bar−1) and 1.85 times of the TFN-GQDs membrane (14.78 LMH·bar−1). Moreover, the TFN membranes incorporating AA-fGQDs with low bandgap energies exhibit better self-cleaning performance than those incorporating AA-fGQDs with high bandgap energies. The TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane shows the best self-cleaning performance with the highest water flux recovery (96.95%), mainly ascribed to the low bandgap energy of Cys-GQDs. Therefore, the desalination and self-cleaning performance of NF membranes can be effectively regulated by the types of AA-fGQDs. It also suggests that the utilization of suitable AA-fGQDs presents a novel and promising strategy to develop self-cleaning NF membranes with excellent desalination performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To develop self-cleaning nanofiltration (NF) membranes with excellent desalination performance, five kinds of amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots (AA-fGQDs) were synthesized and introduced into the active layers of the thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes via the interfacial polymerization (IP) method. The synthesized AA-fGQDs and the resulting TFN NF membranes were systematically characterized to investigate the structure-performance relationships between the AA-fGQDs and the NF membranes. The results showed that the desalination and photocatalytic self-cleaning performance of the fabricated TFN membranes were closely related to the types and structures of AA-fGQDs. The TFN membranes incorporating AA-fGQDs with the polar amino acids exhibit better desalination performance than those incorporating AA-fGQDs with the non-polar amino acids, overcoming the trade-off effect between water flux and salt rejection. The TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane exhibits superior water permeability of 27.35 LMH·bar−1 while maintaining high salt rejection (97.95% for Na2SO4), equal to 4.41 times of the TFC-blank membrane (6.20 LMH·bar−1) and 1.85 times of the TFN-GQDs membrane (14.78 LMH·bar−1). Moreover, the TFN membranes incorporating AA-fGQDs with low bandgap energies exhibit better self-cleaning performance than those incorporating AA-fGQDs with high bandgap energies. The TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane shows the best self-cleaning performance with the highest water flux recovery (96.95%), mainly ascribed to the low bandgap energy of Cys-GQDs. Therefore, the desalination and self-cleaning performance of NF membranes can be effectively regulated by the types of AA-fGQDs. It also suggests that the utilization of suitable AA-fGQDs presents a novel and promising strategy to develop self-cleaning NF membranes with excellent desalination performance.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been an increasing global concern for water resources’ availability [1]. Membrane separation technologies, such as thin-film composite (TFC) nanofiltration (NF) membranes, provide an essential economic tool for addressing water scarcity and sustainable societal development [2]. Nevertheless, there are still two critical challenges for the TFC NF membranes. On the one hand, the trade-off effect between rejection and flux limits the membrane separation efficiency [3]. On the other hand, membrane fouling seriously reduces the service life and dramatically increases the operation cost.

To overcome the trade-off effect and alleviate membrane fouling, the blending method is commonly used by introducing hydrophilic nanofillers into polymer membrane matrix (Psf, PES, PVDF, etc.) [4–7]. However, the blending method can hardly control the uniform distribution of nanofillers in the polymer membrane matrix. The second approach is to attach nanofillers to the membrane surface by surface grafting technique [8–10], which is complicated and time-consuming and faces the challenges of losing nanofillers from the membrane surface. The third approach is embedding nanofillers into the active layer by interfacial polymerization (IP) process, which is facile and efficient to improve separation performance with excellent stability [11–13].

In recent years, nanofiller photocatalysts (e.g., MOFs, TiO2, and β-FeOOH) have been utilized to construct self-cleaning membranes for enhancing antifouling abilities [14–16]. Unfortunately, the conventional nanofillers with photocatalytic properties are incompatible with IP reactions because of the poor dispersion of nanofillers in the aqueous solution containing monomeric amine and the organic solutions containing acid chloride.

As a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit remarkable quantum confinement effect, quantum size effect, and fluorescence properties. It has outstanding advantages such as stronger hydrophilicity, easy synthesis, and excellent biocompatibility [17–19]. Thus GQDs have drawn attention to fabricating high-performance TFN NF membranes owing to the perfect compatibility with the IP process [20,21]. It's found that the separation performance of TFN NF membranes is significantly affected by the functionalized groups (carboxyl, amino, and sulfonic acid groups) of GQDs [22]. Meanwhile, Shao et al. [23] utilized GQDs' photocatalytic abilities towards organic dyes to develop a self-cleaning NF membrane by constructing GQDs/polydopamine interlayer between the PES substrate and the active layer.

Therefore, inspired by the finding that the functionalized groups of the modified GQDs exhibit significant influences on the photocatalytic properties [24] and the separation performance of the NF membranes, in this study, we synthesized five kinds of amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots (AA-fGQDs) using the typical amino acids (Table 1 ). Then, AA-fGQDs were incorporated into the active layer of the TFN membranes via IP method. The fabricated TFN NF membranes were systematically characterized by the membrane surface composition, microstructure, membrane hydrophilicity, desalination performance, and self-cleaning performance. It is expected to develop self-cleaning NF membranes by revealing the structure-performance relationships between AA-fGQDs and self-cleaning NF membranes.

The polysulfone (PSU) ultrafiltration (UF) membrane with a pure water flux of 400 LMH·bar−1 was made by our laboratory. Citric acid (CA, ＞99.8%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.8%), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, 99.8%), and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, 99.8%) were purchased from Xilong (China). L-glycine (98%), L-cysteine (98%), and L-arginine (98%) were purchased from Dalian Meilun (China). L-tryptophan (98%) and L-aspartic acid (98%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin (China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, >99.8%) and sodium chloride (NaCl, >99.8%) were purchased from Sinopharm (China). Piperazine (PIP, 99%) and 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl chloride (TMC, 98%) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean (China). Methyl blue aqueous solution (MB, 1%) was provided by Sigma-Aldrich (China). A laboratory reverse osmosis system was used to prepare pure water.

GQDs and AA-fGQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal synthesis method [25], as shown in Fig. 1 . 10 g CA and the desired amount of amino acids (molar ratio of 1:1) were dissolved in 30 mL pure water by ultrasonication. The mixed solution was heated to 200 °C for 120 min. After that, the reaction solution was neutralized with 0.1 M NaOH. The neutralized solution was purified by the dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da). Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried. GQDs and AA-fGQDs were prepared in the same way except for the different raw materials. The raw material of GQDs was CA, and the raw materials of AA-fGQDs included CA and specific amino acids, as listed in Table 1.

The self-fabricated PSU UF membranes were used as the substrate of the NF membranes [28,29]. Based on our previous study [30], 2000 ppm GQDs or AA-fGQDs were first dissolved in an aqueous PIP solution (0.1% w/v) and ultrasonicated for 30 min to ensure an utterly homogeneous dispersion. Subsequently, the aqueous solution was poured onto the top of the PSU substrate and drained 20 min after exposure to the substrate surface. After drying in the air, the organic phase solution (0.1% w/v TMC/hexane solution) was poured onto the top of the substrate for IP reaction. The remaining organic phase solution was removed after 2 min. The nascent NF membranes were further heated at 60 °C for 20 min. The membrane definitions and the critical IP reaction conditions are listed in Table S1, and the mechanisms of IP reaction are illustrated in Fig. 2 .

The chemical structures of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were characterized by Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Tensor27, Switzerland) in the range of 4000-450 cm−1. The zeta potentials of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were measured by particle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments, Nano Zetasizer 90, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs and AGQDs were obtained by UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 750S, US). The morphology and dimensions of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 1400, Japan). The excitation and emission spectra of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were analyzed by a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS980, UK). The fluorescent images of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were obtained using a fluorescence microscope (Leica, DM6 B, Germany).

The chemical compositions of membrane surfaces were analyzed by attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Bruker Tensor27, Switzerland) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, UK). The fluorescent images of the fabricated membranes were recorded by fluorescence microscopy (Leica, DM6 B, Germany). The membrane morphologies were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS, Sigma, Germany). The surface roughness was measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanosurf, FlexAFM, Switzerland). The membrane hydrophilicity was determined by a contact angle goniometer (HARKE SPCAX3, Beijing, China). The zeta potentials of membranes surfaces were measured using a SurPASS electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of GQDs and AA-fGQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of GQDs and AA-fGQDs on MB was measured under the irradiation of 75 W white LEDs. Specifically, 1 mg GQDs or AA-fGQDs powder was added into a 20 ppm MB aqueous solution. The mixed solution was first placed in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium, which was subsequently irradiated under the LED lamp for 30 min. The photocatalytic abilities of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were assessed based on the degradation rate of MB. The degradation rate (D) of MB is calculated by equation (1):
(1)
D =
(
1 −
C a
C b
)
× 100 %
where C
b and C
a refer to the MB concentration before and after irradiation, respectively. The MB concentration was determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, UV-1780, Japan) at 595 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desalination performance of the fabricated membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The water flux and salt rejection of the fabricated membranes were measured using the laboratory membrane separation devices. The tested membranes (24 cm2) were pre-pressurized and operated under 6 bar and 25 °C. The water flux is calculated by equation (2)：
(2)
F =
V
A · Δt
where F is the water flux (L·m−2 h−1, LMH), V is the volume (L) of permeate, A is the effective membrane area (24 cm2), and Δt is the operation time (h).

The rejection (R) is calculated by equation (3):
(3)
R =
(
1 −
C p
C f
)
× 100 %
where C
p and C
f are the concentrations of the permeate and concentrate, respectively. The salt concentrations were measured by the conductivity meter (Mettler Toledo S3, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-cleaning performance of the fabricated membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic self-cleaning experiment of the fabricated membranes were carried out using a 100 ppm aqueous MB solution. After filtering the MB aqueous solution, the contaminated membranes were placed in a beaker with 100 mL of pure water. The beaker was placed in the dark for 30 min and was irradiated under a 75 W LED white light lamp for 3 h. After the photocatalytic experiment, the water flux and MB rejection were remeasured to evaluate the self-cleaning properties of the tested membranes. The optimal TFN membrane and TFC-blank membrane were selected to investigate the self-cleaning stability via three filtration and self-cleaning cycles. The MB rejection was obtained by measuring MB concentrations in the permeate and concentrate according to Equation (3).

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), both the GQDs and AA-fGQDs exhibit the characteristic absorption peaks at 3457 cm−1, 1586 cm−1, 1258 cm−1, and 1397 cm−1, which are attributed to O–H, CO, C–O stretching vibrations, and OC–O asymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively [22]. Compared with GQDs, five kinds of AA-fGQDs show the enhanced absorption peak at 3300 cm−1 due to their asymmetric stretching vibration of N–H on the edge of AA-fGQDs [26,31]. Meanwhile, the new peak at 1706 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the acid amide bond at the edge of AA-fGQDs [32]. For Cys-GQDs, the new absorption peak at 2547 cm−1 is attributed to the S–H stretching vibration [33], indicating the successful formation of Cys-GQDs. For Arg-GQDs, the new peak at 1690 cm−1 is attributed to the NC stretching vibration, and the broadening peak at 1706 cm−1 is attributed to more acid amide bonds attached to the edge of Arg-GQDs, which also indicates the successful formation of Arg-GQDs.

Because the pH of the aqueous PIP solution during the IP process was approximately 10, the zeta potentials of GQDs and AA-fGQDs were measured at the pH of 7 and 10, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). Since both GQDs and AA-fGQDs contain a large number of carboxyl groups, they exhibit negative electrical properties under the pH condition of 7 and 10 [34]. The Asp-GQDs show the most negatively charged zeta potential under the pH of 10, due to the increased number of dissociated carboxyl groups from Asp-GQDs during aspartic acid incorporation [30].

Fig. 3(c) shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of GQDs, Cys-GQDs, Arg-GQDs, and Asp-GQDs, which reflect their ability of the optical response. The GQDs and the three kinds of AA-fGQDs show strong absorption in the UV region, and the tail of the adsorption peak extends into the visible region. Compared to GQDs, the absorption peaks of AA-fGQDs show a remarkable redshift. The absorption in the 200–300 nm range of GQDs and AA-fGQDsns attributed to the π-π* transitions of sp
2 domains [35]. The absorption of all the measured GQDs and AA-fGQDs in the 300–400 nm range is attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and the n-π* transitions of CO [36].

According to the Kubelka-Munk rule, the bandgap energy could be obtained from the (αhν)2 versus hν plots. As shown in Fig. 3 (d), the bandgap energy order is GQDs (2.58 eV) > Cys-GQDs (1.78 eV) > Arg-GQDs (1.70 eV) > Asp-GQDs (1.43 eV). The decrease of the bandgap energy enhances the visible response of AA-fGQDs and improves the light absorption capacity of AA-fGQDs. Thus, it is expected to enhance the corresponding photocatalytic ability of AA-fGQDs. Moreover, the bandgap energy variation may be related to the functional group of AA-fGQDs [37]. Notably, the edge-connected electron-donating chemical groups of AA-fGQDs introduce n-orbitals between π and π* orbitals, resulting in lower bandgap energies of AA-fGQDs [24].

GQDs/AA-fGQDs present tiny sizes within 16 nm (Fig. S1), indicating that the amino acid type has little influence on the nanoparticle size. However, the fluorescent properties of GQDs/AA-fGQDs are significantly affected by the amino acid type. The Photoluminescence (PL) origin of GQDs includes intrinsic state emission and defect state emission [37–39]. The intrinsic state emission derives from the conjugated π-π* structures of GQDs because of the sp
2 hybridization. The defect state emission results from the functional groups at the edges of GQDs, which affects the corresponding electron leap and luminescence [38]. Fig. 4 shows the images of GQDs and AA-fGQDs nanofillers observed by fluorescence microscopy using different excitation wavelengths. GQDs and AA-fGQDs demonstrate specific fluorescent characteristics dependent upon their surface structures and carbon core sizes [40]. The fluorescent spectra of GQDs and AA-fGQDs are shown in Fig. 5 . According to Fig. 5, most of GQDs/AA-fGQDs except Try-GQDs and Cys-GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior. Their fluorescent emission peaks are red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength, attributed to different sizes and distributions of emission sites [41,42]. The Try-GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent and excitation-independent fluorescent characteristics while the excitation wavelength is above 400 nm and within 300–380 nm, respectively. The excitation-independent of Try-GQDs at 300–380 nm may be attributed to the relatively uniform and passivated surface of Try-GQDs [43]. The Cys-GQDs exhibit excitation-independent fluorescent characteristics due to the S element from Cys-GQDs, which contributes to a uniform and passivated surface [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fabricated NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The ATR-FTIR spectra of the fabricated membranes are shown in Fig. 6 . The weak absorption peak at 1373 cm−1 is attributed to the C–N stretching vibration of the amide group. Meanwhile, the 1664 cm−1 and 1583 cm−1 peaks are attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the amide group (amide I) and N–H bending vibration of the amide group (amide II), which confirms the formation of the polypiperazineamide (PPA) active layer [22]. Compared with the TFC-blank membrane, the TFN membranes show stronger O–H stretching vibration absorption peaks at 3440 cm−1, caused by the abundant oxygen-containing groups from GQDs and AA-fGQDs.

XPS has higher sensitivity than ATR-FTIR for material characterization and was used to quantitatively analyze the chemical composition of the active layers. Fig. 7 (a) shows the full XPS spectra of the TFC-blank and TFN membranes. The binding energies at 284.6 eV, 530.3 eV, 399.0 eV, and 167.3 eV correspond to C, O, N and S, respectively. According to Table 2 , it is clear that the TFN membranes possess a higher O content than the TFC-blank membrane, which results from the large amounts of oxygen-containing groups of the embedded GQDs or AA-fGQDs. In this study, GQDs and AA-fGQDs also contain C, O, and N elements. Thus, the crosslinking degree of the nanocomposite active layer cannot be calculated from the O/C and O/N ratios. Our previous study [30] found that the introductions of GQDs/AA-fGQDs in the aqueous phase solution have two significant effects on the crosslinking degree of the nanocomposite active layers. First, the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between GQDs/AA-fGQDs and PIP during the aqueous deposition stage lead to more PIP adsorption on the membrane surface, which increases the crosslinking degree of the active layer. Second, the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction between GQDs/AA-fGQDs and PIP during the water-oil interface polymerization decrease the diffusion rate of PIP [44], which leads to the reduction of crosslinking in the active layer.

Fig. 7(b)–(h) shows the fitted curves of the N 1s spectra of the fabricated NF membranes. The fitted peaks at 401.0 eV, 399.0 eV, and 397.0 eV are attributed to the secondary amine, tertiary amine, and primary amine. For the TFC-blank membrane, the N element exists in secondary and tertiary amines. After introducing AA-fGQDs into the active layer matrix, some amino groups at the edge of AA-fGQDs react with TMC to form –NH–, and there are still unreacted amino groups. As shown in Fig. 7(d)–(h), all the TFN membranes demonstrate the fitted peak at 397.0 eV ascribed to –NH2, suggesting that AA-fGQDs were successfully incorporated into the nanocomposite active layers. It is worth noting that most of the N element exists in tertiary amine for the TFC-blank and TFN membranes, which corresponds to the complete reaction between PIP and TMC and ensures the high crosslinking degree of the active layer. Meanwhile, the TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes present higher –NH– contents than the TFC-blank membrane because of the reaction between the –NH2 of AA-fGQDs and the chloride group of TMC [45].

Fig. 8 shows the fluorescent images of the fabricated membranes under different excitation wavelengths. The TFC-blank membrane shows no fluorescence. However, all the TFN membranes demonstrate a significant fluorescent feature, suggesting that GQDs and AA-fGQDs were successfully incorporated into the PPA layer [46]. Because the incorporated Cys-GQDs nanofillers have the most pronounced fluorescent characteristic (Fig. 4 (d)), the TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane exhibits the most remarkable fluorescent property under the wavelength of 515–561 nm and 340–380 nm (Fig. 8(e)). Meanwhile, the surfaces of TFN membranes show uniform brightness, indicating that GQDs and AA-fGQDs are uniformly distributed in the active layers.

The surface images in Fig. 9 show that the TFC-blank and TFN membranes have the typical surface morphologies with discrete nodular structures. However, the TFN membranes have thinner active layers than the TFC-blank membrane (311.7 ± 10.4 nm). It is well-known that the IP reaction rate mainly depends on the diffusion rate of PIP monomers. Thus the diffusion rate variation of PIP monomers could decide the final microstructures of the active layer [47]. After introducing GQDs or AA-fGQDs into the aqueous phase during the IP process, the hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between GQDs/AA-fGQDs and PIP lead to the lower PIP diffusion rate, which facilitates the formation of thinner active layers. In addition, the active layer thickness of the TFN membranes is closely related to the physicochemical properties of AA-fGQDs. For example, tryptophan attached to the edge of Try-GQDs is a non-polar amino acid. Thus, the interaction between Try-GQDs and PIP is relatively weak, and Try-GQDs have a less retarding effect on the diffusion rate of PIP monomers. As a result, the TFN-Try-GQDs membrane has a thicker active layer than other TFN membranes. On the contrary, the interactions between Arg-GQDs/Asp-GQDs and PIP are stronger because of the introduction of hydrophilic arginine or aspartic acid to the edges of AA-fGQDs. Accordingly, the retarding effects of Arg-GQDs or Asp-GQDs on the PIP diffusion are enhanced, and the TFN-Arg-GQDs and TFN-Asp-GQDs membranes show thinner active layers.

Fig. 10 shows the membrane surface morphologies of the fabricated membranes, and Table 3 lists the corresponding surface roughness. Compared with the TFC-blank membrane (12.44 nm), the TFN membranes display smoother surfaces due to the introduction of GQDs or AA-fGQDs, which is consistent with the SEM images.

The water contact angle (WCA) of the porous membranes is decided by the hydrophilicity, pore size, porosity, and roughness of the membrane surface. However, for NF membranes having nanometer pore sizes and smooth surfaces, the effects of pore size, porosity, and surface roughness on WCA are often negligible. Thus, WCA is used to directly evaluate the surface hydrophilicity of the fabricated NF membranes in this study (Fig. 11 ). The TFC-blank membrane has the largest WCA of 51.94°. After the embedding of GQDs/AA-fGQDs into the active layers, the WCA of the TFN membranes is dramatically decreased, suggesting the enhanced hydrophilicity of the fabricated NF membranes. This primarily results from the abundant hydrophilic groups of GQDs/AA-fGQDs incorporated in the top active layer. Notably, the type of amino acids in AA-fGQDs has a critical influence on the WCA of the TFN membranes. The TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane exhibits the lowest WCA of 38.67° because aspartic acid is a hydrophilic amino acid with two carboxyl groups. The TFN-Gly-GQDs membrane exhibits the highest WCA of 43.60° because glycine is a non-polar amino acid. Similarly, the TFN-Try-GQDs membrane also shows a higher WCA due to tryptophan's hydrophobic structure (a phenyl ring and a pyrrole ring).

As shown in Fig. 12 . (a), the water fluxes of the fabricated membranes follow the order of TFN-Asp-GQDs > TFN-Gly-GQDs > TFN-Arg-GQDs > TFN-Cys-GQDs > TFN-GQDs > TFN-Try-GQDs > TFC-blank. The TFN membranes show dramatically higher water fluxes than the TFC-blank membrane (6.20 LMH·bar−1) without reducing salt rejections. Generally, the water flux variations of TFC membranes are closely related to the thickness, hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties, crosslinking degree, and roughness of the active layer. Researchers have developed various approaches to manipulate the active layer's morphology by regulating the diffusion rate of the PIP through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and steric hindrance between aqueous additives (PVAs and GOQDs) and PIP [44,48]. As discussed above, the introduction of GQDs or AA-fGQDs facilitates the formation of thinner, smoother, and more hydrophilic active layers with extra water channels, contributing to the significant increment of water fluxes for the TFN membranes. On the one hand, the thinner active layer shortens the traveling distance of water molecules across the membrane [49]. On the other hand, the smooth and hydrophilic membrane surface can further reduce the resistance of water molecules across the membrane [50]. Additionally, the introduction of amino acid promotes more AA-fGQDs covalently incorporated into the active layer, which provides extra water channels [45]. As a result, the TFN membranes demonstrate significantly improved water fluxes.

Many studies reveal that the types of nanofillers incorporated into the active layer via the IP process are essential to membrane permeability [22,51]. In this study, the membrane permeabilities are also significantly affected by the type of AA-fGQDs. The introductions of Asp-GQDs or Arg-GQDs promote the formation of thinner and more hydrophilic active layers. Thus, the water fluxes of the TFN-Asp-GQDs and TFN-Arg-GQDs membrane reach 27.35 LMH·bar−1 and 24.77 LMH·bar−1, which corresponds to 4.41 times and 4.00 times of the TFC-blank membrane, respectively. Although the TFN-Asp-GQDs and TFN-Arg-GQDs membranes have similar active layer thickness, the TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane has a higher water flux because of its stronger hydrophilicity. In addition, the TFN-Gly-GQDs membrane exhibits a relatively high WCA, and it still has a high water flux of 25.10 LMH·bar−1 due to the thinnest active layer (91.8 ± 3.5 nm). In contrast, the TFN-Try-GQDs membrane has a lower WCA than the TFN-Gly-GQDs membrane, but it has a remarkably low water flux of 11.34 LMH·bar−1 due to the thicker active layer (151.0 ± 3.3 nm). Therefore, in this study, both membrane hydrophilicity and active layer thickness are critical for membrane permeability.

As illustrated in Fig. 12(b), regardless of the type of the membranes, the rejection of four salts follows the order of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl, which is consistent with the desalination performance of the negatively charged NF membranes [52,53]. Most importantly, the TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes exhibit significantly improved water flux without reducing salt rejection. The TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes even show higher Na2SO4 and NaCl rejection than the TFC-blank membrane. Commonly, the desalination performance of NF membranes is affected by the size sieving effect and the Donnan exclusion effect [54]. On the one hand, introducing AA-fGQDs into aqueous phase solutions can increase the adsorption of PIP and manipulate the PIP diffusion behavior. As a result, more PIP monomers are involved in the IP reaction, which promotes the generation of the denser active layer with high salt rejection. On the other hand, the embedded AA-fGQDs in the active layers affect the zeta potentials of the TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes (Fig. S2), which matches well with the zeta potentials of AA-fGQDs nanoparticles (Fig. 3(b)). The introductions of Cys-GQDs and Arg-GQDs enhance the electronegativities of the membrane surfaces. The more negatively charged membrane surfaces would generate a stronger electrostatic repulsion towards SO4 2− and a stronger electrostatic attraction towards Mg2+. Therefore, compared with the TFC-blank membrane, the TFN-Cys-GQDs and TFN-Arg-GQDs membranes present the enlarged difference between Na2SO4 rejection and MgSO4 rejection. Although the TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane has a less negatively charged membrane surface, the TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane still exhibits high salt rejections, mainly resulting from its dense active layer.

The stability of the fabricated NF membranes was further evaluated under a continuously cross-flow filtration for 10 h. According to Fig. S3, both the TFC-blank and TFN membranes exhibit stable permeance and sodium sulfate rejection. It suggests that the incorporated AA-fGQDs nanoparticles demonstrate excellent compatibility with the PPA matrix, maintaining the mechanical strength of the TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes.

As shown in Fig. 13 and Table S2, the fabricated TFN-Asp-GQDs, TFN-Arg-GQDs, and TFN-Cys-GQDs membranes exhibit superior desalination performance with remarkably higher permeation than the TFN NF membranes reported in the recent literature. However, the desalination performance improvements of the TFN-GQDs, TFN-Try-GQDs, and TFN-Gly-GQDs membranes are not significant. Therefore, the desalination performance of the TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes is closely related to the amino acid types of the AA-fGQDs nanoparticles. The TFN membranes using the pristine GQDs and the AA-fGQDs nanoparticles with non-polar amino acids (Try-GQDs and Gly-GQDs) showed poor improvements. Most importantly, the AA-fGQDs nanoparticles incorporating polar amino acids (Asp-GQDs, Arg-GQDs, and Cys-GQDs) contribute to extraordinary improvements by constructing thin, hydrophilic, smooth, and dense active layers with additional water channels. Therefore, utilizing suitable AA-fGQDs could successfully overcome the trade-off effect between water flux and salt rejection.

The dye MB is used to evaluate the photocatalytic abilities of GQDs and AA-fGQDs. As shown in Fig. 14 (a), compared to the blank solution without nanofillers, the colors of MB solutions change considerably while GQDs or AA-fGQDs were introduced as photocatalysts. This result is consistent with the UV–vis analysis in Fig. 14(b). Apparently, five kinds of AA-fGQDs exhibit better photocatalytic abilities than GQDs. Because Asp-GQDs have the lowest bandgap energy, Asp-GQDs demonstrate superior photocatalytic ability over the other AA-fGQDs and have the highest degradation rate of MB (88%). Additionally, Cys-GQDs and Arg-GQDs have similar photocatalytic capabilities of degrading 72% MB. The mechanisms of photocatalytic degradation with the help of GQDs/AA-fGQDs are illustrated in Fig. 15 [55]. First, GQDs/AA-fGQDs capture photons of visible light (with energy satisfying hv ≥ Eg), after which electrons (e−) in the valence band absorb the energy in the photons and leap to the conduction band where holes (h+) are created in the valence band. After that, the generated e− and h+ migrate to the surfaces of GQDs/AA-fGQDs. e− with reducing ability and h+ with oxidizing ability can react with O2 and H2O to form the superoxide radical (·O2 −) and the hydroxyl radical (·OH−), respectively. The resultant ·O2 − and ·OH− undergo redox reactions with MB around GQDs/AA-fGQDs, generating O2, H2O, and other degradation products.

Fig. 16 (a) displays the color change on the membrane surfaces before and after the light irradiation. Compared to the TFC membrane, more blue areas were removed from the surfaces of the TFN membranes after the light irradiation. According to the normalized water flux and MB rejection in Fig. 16(b), the TFN membranes exhibit superior self-cleaning performance to the TFC-blank membrane. The introduction of GQDs and AA-fGQDs empowers the NF membrane with the photocatalytic degradation abilities towards MB. Moreover, except for the TFN-Gly-GQDs membrane, the TFN-AA-fGQDs membranes exhibit better photocatalytic self-cleaning performance than the TFN-GQDs membrane, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 14. The TFN-Cys-GQDs and TFN-Asp-GQDs membranes exhibit excellent photocatalytic self-cleaning performance, demonstrating the almost complete water flux recovery (96.96%) after irradiation. It is attributed to the low bandgap energies of Asp-GQDs and Cys-GQDs, facilitating electron transition and thus enhancing photocatalytic activity [23]. In contrast, the high bandgap energy of GQDs is negative for electron leap, and thus the TFN-GQDs membrane exhibits relatively poor self-cleaning performance.

The photocatalytic self-cleaning performance of the TFN membranes is related to the inherent photocatalytic properties of AA-fGQDs nanofillers and the membrane morphologies. According to Fig. 14, Arg-GQDs and Cys-GQDs nanofillers have equivalent photocatalytic abilities. Nevertheless, the TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane has a smoother membrane surface than the TFN-Arg-GQDs membrane. As a result, the TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane exhibits better self-cleaning performance than the TFN-Arg-GQDs membrane because more MB pollutants are prone to be trapped on the rougher surface [56]. Furthermore, the optimal TFN membrane (TFN-Cys-GQDs) and TFC-blank membranes are selected to compare their self-cleaning stabilities. After three filtration and self-cleaning cycles, the fouling of the TFC-blank membrane becomes heavier, but the TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane still retains an excellent self-cleaning ability (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6).

In this study, five kinds of AA-fGQDs were successfully synthesized and incorporated into the active layer to fabricate high-performance NF membranes. The structure-performance relationships between AA-fGQDs and NF membranes were investigated systematically. The results show that the desalination and photocatalytic self-cleaning performance of the fabricated NF membranes are closely related to the types and structures of AA-fGQDs. The TFN membranes incorporating AA-fGQDs with polar amino acids exhibit better desalination performance than those incorporating AA-fGQDs with non-polar amino acids. Meanwhile, the use of appropriate AA-fGQDs could successfully overcome the trade-off effect between water flux and salt rejection. The TFN-Asp-GQDs membrane exhibits superior water permeability of 27.35 LMH·bar−1 while maintaining high salt rejection (97.95% for Na2SO4), equal to 4.41 times of the TFC-blank membrane and 1.85 times of the TFN-GQDs membrane. Additionally, the TFN membranes containing AA-fGQDs with low bandgap energies exhibit excellent self-cleaning performance than those containing AA-fGQDs with high bandgap energies. The TFN-Cys-GQDs membrane shows the best self-cleaning performance with complete water flux recovery because of the low bandgap energy of Cys-GQDs and the smooth and hydrophilic membrane surface. The findings of this work show that the utilization of suitable AA-fGQDs is a novel and promising strategy to develop self-cleaning NF membranes with excellent desalination performance.
